1: Ezekiel was a priest who was deported to Babylon in the first deportation (597 BCE) and received his call-vision in 593 BCE by the Chebar canal in Babylonia ('the thirtieth year', Ezekiel 1:1 — possibly his own thirtieth year, the age for priestly service). He prophesied to the exilic community ca. 593-571 BCE. His priestly background shapes his theology: the book is preoccupied with divine glory (kavod), the departure of the Shekinah from the temple (Ezekiel 8-11), and its eschatological return (Ezekiel 40-48). The merkabah vision (Ezekiel 1) was the most influential single vision in subsequent Jewish mysticism — the Hekhalot literature built an entire tradition of heavenly ascent around it. The four living creatures (lion, ox, eagle, human) reappear in Irenaeus's identification of the four Gospel symbols.
2: The parenthetical date-note (Ezekiel 1:2-3) anchors the vision historically: the fifth year of Jehoiachin's exile = 593 BCE. Jehoiachin (Jeconiah) had been deported with the second wave in 597 BCE (2 Kings 24:10-16) along with 10,000 skilled workers and warriors. He remained a recognized king in Babylonian records — the Weidner Tablets (discovered in Babylon) list rations issued to 'Yaukin, king of Judah' and his sons, confirming his quasi-royal status in exile. The Babylonian exile was not a prison but an administrative relocation; deportees like Ezekiel could own property, build houses, and conduct business (as Jeremiah 29:4-7 counsels). This context matters: Ezekiel's audience was not enslaved and desperate but economically active and tempted to assimilate.
3: Ezekiel's dual identity as priest and prophet (kohen and navi) is unusual — only Jeremiah shares this combination among the writing prophets. His priestly formation shaped everything: the book's obsession with the temple, the divine glory (kavod), holiness distinctions, and the liturgical calendar. The River Chebar (Hebrew nahar K'bar) has been identified with the nar Kabari in Babylonian administrative texts — a canal near Nippur in southern Mesopotamia where a Jewish deportee community is attested. The phrase “the hand of the LORD was upon him” (yad YHWH aleiv) is Ezekiel's characteristic marker for the onset of the ecstatic visionary state (also Ezekiel 3:14, 22; Ezekiel 8:1; Ezekiel 33:22; Ezekiel 37:1; Ezekiel 40:1); it is closely paralleled in Elijah (1 Kings 18:46) and Elisha (2 Kings 3:15) but appears more frequently in Ezekiel than in any other prophet.
4: The theophanic approach from the north is charged with polemical significance. In Canaanite mythology, Baal's divine mountain (Zaphon) was located to the north — Psalms 48:2 appropriates this tradition by identifying Zion with “the heights of Zaphon.” Storm-theophanies from the north signal divine approach in the warrior-god tradition. Crucially, Ezekiel is in Babylon when the northward storm arrives — implying that YHWH is not geographically confined to Jerusalem or Canaan. This directly refutes the Babylonian theological assumption that deporting a people means defeating their god: YHWH's throne-chariot is mobile and present in exile. The “gleaming metal” (hashmal) in the fire-center is a hapax legomenon whose precise meaning is unknown — possibly electrum (gold-silver alloy) or a supernatural luminescent substance. The LXX translates it elektron; later rabbinic tradition treated it as a divine energy so dangerous that unauthorized exposition of the merkabah chapter required the teacher to be surrounded by fire.
5: The four living creatures (hayyot, literally “living ones”) are identified as cherubim (keruvim) in Ezekiel 10 — not the soft-winged infant figures of later Christian art but the fierce guardian-beings of the ANE. Composite figures with multiple faces appear throughout ancient Near Eastern royal iconography: Assyrian lamassu (winged human-headed bulls protecting palace entrances), Babylonian apkallu (fish-sages with divine wisdom), Egyptian sphinxes. Ezekiel's hayyot are recognizably within this tradition but transformed: they serve YHWH's throne, not a pagan king's palace. The original audience in Babylon, surrounded by these monumental composite figures on every temple gateway, would have understood the visual claim immediately — YHWH's attendants are superior to Babylon's guardian figures.
6: Four faces and four wings are specified here (cf. Isaiah's seraphim who have six wings, Isaiah 6:2; Revelation's living creatures also have six wings, Revelation 4:8 — suggesting Ezekiel's count differs from the heavenly court in other visions). The number four in ANE cosmography represents the four cardinal directions and hence universality — the four-faced, four-winged creatures embody YHWH's omnidirectional sovereignty, not limited to any single spatial orientation.
7: “Straight legs” (possibly meaning unbent, single-joint) and “a calf's hoof” — bovine feet signal the ox-face component of the composite being. The gleaming bronze (nechoshet qalal, burnished bronze) evokes the bronze sea and bronze pillars of Solomon's temple (1 Kings 7:15-16, 23-26). Priestly temple-knowledge would make these associations immediate for Ezekiel: the hayyot carry the vocabulary of the temple into Babylonian exile, asserting continuity between YHWH's earthly dwelling and his heavenly throne.
8: Human hands beneath their wings represent capacity for purposeful action — distinguishing these beings from mere natural forces. The fourfold symmetry (each face on each side) emphasizes the uniformity and order of YHWH's heavenly court. The absence of asymmetry or imperfection is a theological statement: no chaos, no conflict within the divine retinue — in stark contrast to the conflict-laden Babylonian creation myth (Enuma Elish) where the divine order is achieved through violence and disorder.
9: The wings touching neighbors and straight-ahead movement without turning expresses the absolute purposefulness of the divine chariot's movement. In ANE military contexts, disciplined marching without turning was a mark of elite troops. Here it expresses that YHWH's purposes are not diverted or deflected by external obstacles — the divine chariot moves where the Spirit directs, without the hesitation or recalibration that characterizes human planning.
10: The four faces — human, lion, ox, eagle — have generated extensive interpretation. Irenaeus (2nd century CE) was the first to map them onto the four Gospels (Matthew/human, Mark/lion, Luke/ox, John/eagle), a scheme that became standard in Christian iconography (the symbols of the four Evangelists). In Ezekiel's context, these represent the four dominant animal categories: the most intelligent creature (human), king of wild beasts (lion), king of domestic animals (ox), king of birds (eagle) — together suggesting the totality of the animate creation in service of YHWH's throne. The Babylonian deities had their own animal emblems and were often depicted in composite form; Ezekiel's vision appropriates and transcends this visual vocabulary.
11: The wing-configuration — two spread outward touching neighbors, two covering the body — creates both connection (to the neighboring hayyot) and modesty (covering their own form before the divine presence). The same instinct of covering before YHWH appears in Isaiah's seraphim (Isaiah 6:2: two wings to cover the face, two to cover the feet). Even the highest heavenly beings are not fully unveiled before the divine glory — a consistent theme that relativizes all created beings before YHWH.
12: “Each moved straight ahead — wherever the spirit directed them.” The Hebrew ruach here is probably the divine Spirit rather than simply “wind” — the beings move as the Spirit moves within them. This is consistent with ancient Near Eastern divine-throne theology: the king's divine attendants execute his will with perfect responsiveness. But Ezekiel's point is that the Spirit of YHWH (not the spirits of Babylonian deities) animates the cosmic order.
13: Burning coals and blazing torches moving among the hayyot evoke the theophanic fire of Sinai (Exodus 19:18) and the pillar of fire in the wilderness (Exodus 13:21). Fire is YHWH's signature element in theophany — it is the medium through which the divine presence manifests while remaining unapproachable (Hebrews 12:29: “our God is a consuming fire,” citing Deuteronomy 4:24). Lightning (baraq) flashing from the fire intensifies the storm-theophany motif of Ezekiel 1:4.
14: The darting movement like a lightning flash completes the storm-theophany: wind (Ezekiel 1:4), cloud (Ezekiel 1:4), fire (Ezekiel 1:4, 13), lightning (Ezekiel 1:13, 14). All four classical theophanic elements are present. In Canaanite religion these were Baal's storm attributes; Psalm 29 already repurposed this storm-god imagery for YHWH. Ezekiel does the same but in the context of Babylonian exile, where Marduk (the Babylonian storm-creator deity) is the dominant religious claim. YHWH's throne-chariot arrives in Babylon with all the attributes of divine cosmic sovereignty.
15: The ophanim (wheels) appear beside each of the four hayyot when they land. The wheel-within-wheel design (Ezekiel 1:16) has been compared to gyroscopic mechanisms and astronomical models; the most plausible ancient parallel is the Babylonian astronomical tradition of wheels representing planetary motion. Babylonian astral religion had developed sophisticated mathematical astronomy by the 6th century BCE — the stars and planets were divine beings whose movements determined fate. Ezekiel's vision places a YHWH-controlled mechanism above all this astral machinery: the ophanim are not cosmic fate-wheels but YHWH's chariot-wheels, responsive to the Spirit, not to astrological determinism.
16: “Like beryl” (tarshish) — probably a yellow or golden gemstone; Tarshish-stone appears also in Daniel 10:6. The description “a wheel intersecting a wheel” (ophan betokh haophan) allows omnidirectional movement without turning — a mechanical impossibility in human engineering, serving as a visual marker of the supernatural. The wheels' ability to move in any of the four directions without turning reinforces the omnidirectional sovereignty expressed by the four faces.
17: The capacity to move in any direction without turning was functionally important for ancient military formations (turning a unit exposes flanks). Here it expresses perfect divine freedom of movement — YHWH is not constrained by the geography that constrains human armies. The Babylonian theology that a defeated nation's god could not function outside its territorial homeland is directly refuted: YHWH's throne is mobile and omnidirectionally operative.
18: The “rims full of eyes” is one of the most striking features of the vision and appears again in Revelation 4:6. In ANE iconography, multiple eyes on divine beings symbolize omniscience — the ability to see in all directions simultaneously. The Babylonian deity Marduk was described as “four-eyed,” and the sun-god Shamash (god of justice and judgment) was depicted with eyes everywhere. Ezekiel's ophanim with eye-covered rims claim YHWH's total omniscient surveillance — appropriate for a God whose throne-chariot is present in the land of the enemy.
19: The perfect coordination between hayyot and ophanim — rising together, moving together — establishes the unity of the divine vehicle. Unlike the Babylonian divine assembly where multiple deities have potentially competing agendas, YHWH's heavenly court operates in perfect unity. The exilic community, watching Babylon's elaborate divine processions (the New Year festival featured the barges of multiple deities processing through the city), would recognize what Ezekiel was countering: YHWH's heavenly host is more coherent and more powerful than Babylon's divine bureaucracy.
20: “The spirit of the living creature was in the wheels” (ruach hahayyah baophanim) — the Spirit animates both the living creatures and the wheels as a single unified system. This is not mechanism but life: the divine chariot is a living entity, not a machine. The Babylonian astronomical system was deterministic — planetary movements were calculable fate-machinery. Ezekiel insists YHWH's cosmic governance is personal, Spirit-animated, and responsive to the divine will rather than mechanical and predetermined.
21: The repetition of the coordination formula (Ezekiel 1:19-21) is characteristic of Ezekiel's style: he describes the same feature multiple times from slightly different angles. Scholars have proposed various explanations (scribal additions, literary ring-structure, liturgical repetition). The most likely function is emphatic: the perfect responsiveness of the divine chariot to the divine will is so important theologically that it bears repetition. The exilic community needed to know that YHWH's movements were not random or uncontrolled.
22: The raqia (firmament) stretched above the hayyot echoes Genesis 1:6-8, where the raqia separates the waters above from below. Ezekiel's firmament is “like crystal” (keein haqqarach hanoorah — like the awesome ice/crystal) and hovers between the hayyot and the divine throne. The cosmological structure mirrors the priestly cosmology: below (earth/exile), the hayyot-level (heavenly court), the firmament (boundary of the divine realm), the throne (YHWH's presence). This vertical structure places YHWH definitively above the Babylonian cosmos.
23: Wings spread straight out, two covering the body — the modesty gesture before the divine presence reappears. The precise description of wing-angles in liturgical context suggests priestly vocabulary: the placement and orientation of priestly garments, the arrangement of temple objects, the position of the cherubim in the Holy of Holies (Exodus 25:20) were all regulated with this level of precision. Ezekiel is describing a heavenly temple whose furnishings correspond to and surpass the earthly one.
24: “Like the sound of many waters, like the voice of the Almighty” (kekol mayim rabbim kekol Shaddai) — both phrases are theophanic comparisons. “Many waters” (mayim rabbim) is the OT's characteristic sound for YHWH's voice (Psalms 29:3; Revelation 1:15; Ezekiel 14:2). Shaddai (the Almighty) is an ancient divine name associated particularly with the patriarchal period and the book of Job, where it occurs 31 times. Its sound-comparison here suggests the deep resonance of overwhelming power rather than words. The “great rumbling like the noise of an army” frames YHWH as divine warrior — a title directly applicable to the Babylonian military context of exile.
25: The lowering of wings when the hayyot stand still corresponds to the silence that falls when YHWH speaks from above the firmament. This is theophanic liturgy: when the voice is about to speak, all subsidiary sounds cease. The same pattern appears at Mount Sinai where the people fell silent before the divine voice (Exodus 20:18-19). The vision is structured as a heavenly liturgical assembly that mirrors and exceeds the earthly cult — a crucial assertion for a community whose earthly temple is inaccessible.
26: “Something like a sapphire throne” (keein aven sapir demut kisse) — sapphire (or lapis lazuli) is the characteristic throne-material in ANE divine-council literature. The Sinai elders “saw the God of Israel, and under his feet was something like a pavement of sapphire” (Exodus 24:10). In Mesopotamian literature, lapis lazuli was the material of the heavens themselves — its deep blue represented the sky's vault. Ezekiel's sapphire throne places YHWH above the very heaven that Babylon identified with the divine realm. “A form with a human appearance” (demut kemar-eh adam) above the throne is the most theologically careful part of the vision: not Adam, not an angel, not a named deity — a form that resembles a human figure. This reserve is specifically anti-idolatrous.
27: The description from waist up/waist down in fire and radiance recalls the fire-within-fire of Ezekiel 1:4 but now personalized. The entire vision has been building to this: the fire-storm from the north, the hayyot, the wheels, the firmament — all are the entourage and vehicle of this figure. The refusal to describe the figure more precisely is itself theologically significant: Deuteronomy's prohibition of images (Deuteronomy 4:15-18) was based precisely on the fact that Israel saw no form (temunah) at Sinai. Ezekiel sees a form but describes it only through negation and approximation — a visionary language that preserves the Deuteronomic reserve while asserting genuine divine presence.
28: The rainbow surrounding the divine glory recalls the covenant sign of Genesis 9:13-16 — the first time YHWH appeared in glory surrounded by a rainbow was after the flood, as a covenant promise. Ezekiel sees the rainbow in Babylon: the covenant faithfulness that YHWH displayed to Noah is present even in the place of exile and judgment. This is the hope embedded in the terrifying vision: the God who appears in storm and fire is also the God of the covenant rainbow. The prophet falls on his face — the standard prostration response to divine theophany (cf. Isaiah 6:5; Daniel 8:17; Revelation 1:17) — and hears a voice, which initiates the commission of Ezekiel 2.
